title: Logistic Regression from Scratch
excerpt: Linear regression predicts numbers. But what if you need to predict yes or no? Spam or not spam? That is classification and logistic regression is where it starts. In this post we build it from scratch using only NumPy: sigmoid function, binary cross-entropy loss, gradient descent. No sklearn. No shortcuts.

title: Linear Regression from Scratch
excerpt: Most machine learning courses hand you sklearn and tell you to call .fit(). That is fine for getting things done fast, but it skips the part that actually matters: understanding what the algorithm is doing underneath. In this post, I build linear regression from the ground up using only NumPy forward pass, loss function, gradients, weight updates, all of it written by hand.

title: Linux Init Systems: From SysVinit to systemd and the Community War Nobody Asked For
excerpt: Every time your Linux machine boots, something has to wake everything else up. That something is your init system. the very first process the kernel hands control to. It's quiet, invisible, and almost nobody talks about it. Until they do. And when they do, things get heated.

title: Python for Everyone #Post 03
excerpt: Now that you know variables and data types, it is time to teach your program how to make decisions. In this post, we learn if, elif, and else — the building blocks of logic in Python. By the end, your programs will be able to respond differently depending on what the user says or does.
tags: Python for Everyone a blog series

title: Linux Release Models: Rolling, Stable, and Everything In Between
excerpt: A personal look at how Linux distributions manage software releases and why choosing the right model matters more than choosing the right desktop.

title: Display Server Protocols: X11, Wayland, and Beyond
excerpt: A technical deep dive into how Linux, macOS, Windows, Android, and other platforms handle display rendering and why the protocol wars matter.

title: Python for Everyone #Post 02
excerpt:
Excerpt:
"In this post, you will learn how to store information in Python using variables, understand the four basic data types (int, float, str, bool), do math operations, work with text, and even ask the user for input. By the end, you will build a real shopping bill calculator using nothing but the basics.
tags: Python for Everyone a blog series

title: Linux Desktop Environments: A Personal Journey Through GNOME, KDE, and XFCE
excerpt: After years of hopping between GNOME's polished ecosystem and KDE's endless customization rabbit holes, I finally found my home in XFCE — and I've never looked back. GNOME made me feel like a guest in someone else's vision of computing. KDE turned me into a hobbyist when I just wanted to get work done. XFCE? It simply got out of my way. Fast, stable, and sensible out of the box, it's the desktop that finally let me focus on the work instead of the environment running it.

title: Python for Everyone #Post 01
excerpt:
Hi there! Welcome to Python for Everyone a blog series where we learn Python from
scratch, step by step, all the way up to Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Whether you have never written a single line of code before, or you have tried Python once and felt confused, this series is for you.
tags: Python for Everyone a blog series

title: The Complete Guide to .gitignore
excerpt: A comprehensive beginner-to-advanced guide to Git’s .gitignore file. Learn how .gitignore works, how to create and configure it, understand pattern syntax, avoid common mistakes, and keep sensitive files like API keys and environment variables out of your repository.
